message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 45 (0 todo — ALL tests implemented)
Recent Green phase: No code change — "schema example amulet quote + claim" was a confirming test. The kata is feature-complete: quote (all item types, component blocks, all premium modifiers, rounding) and claim (deductible, special clauses, cap consumption/clamping, payout rounding, validation/rejection) all pass.

This is the final refactor pass. Review the whole implementation file end to end for any remaining naming, duplication, or simplification opportunities now that the full picture is visible. Evaluate naming first, apply the Four Rules, compute APP mass and McCabe for the worst-offender functions before/after. Apply a refactoring only if it genuinely improves clarity/metrics while keeping all 45 tests green; otherwise document why the code is at its floor.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
45/45 passing. ESLint clean (0 smells, no cognitive-complexity flags). Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for the worst-offender functions.

## PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells**: 0. **Cognitive complexity**: nothing flagged (all below SonarJS threshold).

Let me compute McCabe for the structurally larger functions — they are the only candidates.

**McCabe cyclomatic per function** (start 1, +1 per decision):

- `processClaim` — no branches in body (calls do the branching): McCabe 1
- `processQuote` — `> 0` comparison in `isFollowUp`: McCabe 2
- `assertClaimable` — `for` loop + `if` + `??`: McCabe 4 (worst)
- `assertNonNegativeAmounts` — `find` predicate `<` + `if`: McCabe 3
- `runScenario` — ternary `? :`: McCabe 2
- `damageReimbursement` — `&&` + `?:` + `??`: McCabe 4 (worst, tied)
- `itemSurchargeRate` — two `?:` + `??` (via isHighlyEnchanted): McCabe ~3
- `assertKnownItems` — `find` + `if`: McCabe 3

**Worst-offender McCabe = 4** (`assertClaimable`, `damageReimbursement`). Both are low.

**APP mass for the two worst (McCabe 4):**

`assertClaimable`:
```
const insured = countBy(...)      // assignment-binding 1, invocation 2
const claimed = countBy(...)      // binding 1, invocation 2
for (...Object.entries(claimed))  // loop 5, invocation 2
  if (count > (insured[x] ?? 0))  // conditional 4, binding 2, const 1, invocation(??) implicit
    throw new Error(`...`)        // invocation 2, const 1, binding 1
```
Mass ≈ bindings(8)·1 + consts(2)·1 + invocations(~6)·2 + conditional(1)·4 + loop(1)·5 = 8+2+12+4+5 = ~31

`damageReimbursement`: a single expression — bindings ~4, invocation 1, conditional(?:) 4, comparison ≈ Mass ~15.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Reviewing every function name against its current behavior:

- `mainItemPremium`, `mainItems`, `componentTotal`, `policyBase`, `policyDiscount`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `followUpDiscount`, `roundUpToWhole`, `roundDownToWhole`, `quotePremium` — all reveal intent precisely.
- `damageReimbursement` / `damagePayout` — clear pair: reimbursement is pre-deductible, payout subtracts deductible. Good.
- `assertClaimable` / `assertNonNegativeAmounts` / `assertKnownItems` — consistent assert-prefix family, each names its invariant. Good.
- `countBy`, `withSurcharge`, `isComponent`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `findInsuredItem`, `insuranceSum`, `incidentPayout` — all accurate.

No naming defects. The names already reveal intent at a high standard.

## Step 4: Refactoring Assessment

This is the final pass with the full picture visible. Let me check the Four Rules for genuine improvement opportunities:

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: 45/45 green.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Names are precise; the file reads top-to-bottom as small composed functions. No clarity defect.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: The two `policyDiscount` callers (`loyaltyDiscount`, `followUpDiscount`) already factor the shared shape through `policyDiscount(items, rate, applies)`. The three `assert*` functions share a *shape* but each expresses a different invariant with a different message and data source — collapsing them would obscure intent (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3). `countBy` is reused by both `componentCounts` and `assertClaimable`. No actionable duplication remains.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Every helper has at least one caller and a distinct reason to exist. No dead abstraction.

The worst McCabe in the file is 4, and the worst cognitive complexity is below the lint threshold — every function is at or near its floor for the behavior it expresses. The implementation is already a clean composition of single-purpose functions. Any structural change I attempted (e.g., merging the assert family into a generic validator, or inlining `damageReimbursement` into `damagePayout`) would raise mass/cognitive load or destroy a revealing name without lowering any metric.

I'll verify this conviction by trying one candidate: inlining `damageReimbursement` into `damagePayout` to reduce element count (Rule 4). Let me measure whether it helps.

Mentally: merging them produces one function with `&& ?: ?? -` — McCabe stays 4, cognitive rises (one denser expression), mass roughly equal, and it couples "high-enchantment rate" with "deductible subtraction" — two distinct concepts now tangled. That fails Rule 2. Not applying it.

## Decision: No refactoring applied — code is at its floor

```
Refactoring Evaluation (full-file final pass):
- ESLint smells: 0 (clean)
- Cognitive complexity: nothing flagged (all functions below SonarJS threshold)
- Worst McCabe: 4 (assertClaimable, damageReimbursement) — both minimal for their logic
- Naming: every function name reveals current intent; no rename warranted
- Duplication: shared shapes already factored (policyDiscount, countBy, withSurcharge, roundTo); remaining assert-family similarity is intentional (distinct invariants/messages — Rule 2 > Rule 3)
- Fewest elements: every helper has a caller and a distinct purpose; no dead abstraction
```

Reasoning: The file is already decomposed into small, well-named, single-responsibility functions composed through `runScenario`. Each metric sits at or near its floor for the behavior expressed. I considered two concrete changes — merging the three `assert*` functions into a generic validator, and inlining `damageReimbursement` into `damagePayout` — and rejected both: each would tangle distinct concepts and reduce intent-revelation without lowering any metric. Per the discipline, Rule 2 (clarity) trumps both APP mass and McCabe, so no change is justified.

Tests remain 45/45 green; no edits made.

Now writing the done marker since all tests are implemented and passing.

content.text:
Refactoring Complete (final full-file pass):

- **Refactoring**: none applied — code is already at its floor
- **Smell delta**: 0 → 0 (ESLint clean)
- **Cognitive delta**: nothing flagged before or after (all functions below SonarJS threshold)
- **APP mass**: worst offenders `assertClaimable` (~31) and `damageReimbursement` (~15) — both minimal for their logic
- **McCabe delta**: worst-offender McCabe stays at 4 (`assertClaimable`, `damageReimbursement`); no reducible decision points
- **Tests**: 45/45 passing

I measured the whole file end to end. ESLint reports zero violations and no cognitive-complexity flags. I evaluated naming for every function — all names reveal current intent (the `assert*` family, the `damageReimbursement`/`damagePayout` pre/post-deductible pair, and the `*Discount` composition through `policyDiscount` are all precise). I checked the Four Rules: shared shapes are already factored (`policyDiscount`, `countBy`, `withSurcharge`, `roundTo`), the remaining similarity among the three `assert*` functions is intentional (distinct invariants and messages — Rule 2 clarity trumps Rule 3 DRY), and every helper has a caller and a distinct purpose (no dead abstraction).

I considered two concrete changes and rejected both because each would tangle distinct concepts and hurt intent-revelation without lowering any metric: merging the `assert*` trio into a generic validator, and inlining `damageReimbursement` into `damagePayout`.

No edits were made; `experiment-done.txt` written with `DONE`.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_00-18-11_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
